elements.sent-2411-001: In this section, we conducted a brief review of the former attempts that utilize AI Agents for scientific research.
elements.sent-2411-005: AI-Scientist\citep{lu2024aiscientist proposed a fully automated AI driven scientific research framework.}
elements.sent-2411-006: In order to put forward an AI Agent system that can autonomously conduct the end-to-end scientific research, we first reflect on the way human researchers conduct scientific research.
elements.subsubsection-2411-007: Comparison between Human Practice and Existed Systems
elements.sent-2411-007: We follow the practice of human researchers and tried to build our system more human-like.
elements.sent-2411-008: Firstly, we introduce the concept of Domain Specific Language(DSL).
elements.sent-2411-009: In the AIGS system, agents have access to a wide action space, particularly when tasked with interpreting the methodology to the executable actions.
elements.sent-2411-010: For instance, in machine learning tasks, an agent may take actions ranging from editing multiple Python files to altering the format or distribution ratio of input data, as part of the methodology execution.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, in the design of a fully automated system, the unexpected behaviour of the agents must be avoided, and compatibility should be ensured for all the potential actions.
elements.sent-2411-012: To address this challenge, we design DSL to restrict the action space of the agents.
elements.sent-2411-013: Specifically, DSL is as a pre-designed JSON object, tailored to the specific task.
elements.sent-2411-014: This approach allows agents to execute the methodology and conduct experiments by compiling a simple JSON object, thus ensuring a controllable action space for the agents, while allowing them to expediently execute the proposed methodology at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-015: From a higher-level perspective, DSL acts as the bridge that fills the gap between proposal generation and experiment execution.
elements.sent-2411-016: Given the current limitations of model ability, it is unrealistic to expect that agents can fully autonomously carry out the proposed experiment.
elements.sent-2411-017: In practice, we tried asking agents to compile a model training script, while encountered frequent failure, which remained unsolved after iterations.
elements.sent-2411-018: Recognizing that language models excel in natural language, we design DSL System to empower agents with the abilities to manipulate the experiment module in a natural language format, which connects the proposal level generation with practical steps in the experiment module.
elements.sent-2411-019: As the first and the most important step in the scientific research research, idea formation and methodology design usually lay the foundation for valuable insights or impactful discoveries.
elements.sent-2411-020: Given the critical importance of these tasks, a well-designed module is essential to the success of the whole system.
elements.sent-2411-021: We refer to this module as Proposal Agent, drawing inspiration from human practice of drafting a proposal before starting out on the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-022: Proposal Agent is provided with the detailed description of the research topic, a record of previously executed experiments, and the review from Review Agent as described in \ref{sec:reviewagent .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Based on these information, Proposal Agent is tasked with generating the following key components:.
elements.sent-2411-024: Idea serves as a general observation of the task, as well as an outline of the current iteration.
elements.sent-2411-025: It provides an explanation for the experimental results or any phenomenon observed of the task.
elements.sent-2411-026: Idea offers a high-level understanding of the research problem and the potential solutions, guiding the generation of other components within Proposal Agent.
elements.sent-2411-027: Methodology gives a brief but concise description of actions to be carried out in the experiment in natural language.
elements.sent-2411-028: It follows the outline laid out in Idea and make attempts to deal with the related phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-029: Methodology should try either to improve the experiment results or advance towards the discovery of scientific rules.
elements.sent-2411-030: DSL interprets Methodology into system-executable code as elaborated in \ref{sec:dsl .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The structure and content of DSL varies across different tasks and is pre-designed and formatted when composing the prompt in implementation of Proposal Agent.
elements.sent-2411-032: Setting also acts as the parameters and conditions under which the experiment is to be executed.
elements.sent-2411-033: Different from Methodology which determines the primary approach, Setting modifies experiment configurations, such as specifying which iteration should the experiment be build upon, which is a must in our AIGS system called baseline.
elements.sent-2411-034: We emphasize the importance of the Methodology, as it is vital for the ablation study to be conducted in Ablation Agent.
elements.sent-2411-035: In this regard, Setting acts as a key factor towards the more advanced scientific discovery.
elements.sent-2411-036: Hypothesis represents the prediction of Proposal Agent regarding the expected outcomes of the experiment after the execution of the proposed methodology.
elements.sent-2411-037: In our expectation, hypothesis plays a crucial role in enabling Proposal Agent to acquire knowledge: it formulates hypothesis based on the current understanding, evaluates whether the experiment result align with the hypothesis, and then identifies the discrepancies, figuring out the underlying faults that lead to the wrong hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-038: Metric is closely aligned with Hypothesis and outlines the aspects of the experiment results that need to be evaluated in order to verify the validity of the Hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-039: It guides Review Agent in identifying relevant Features and conducting a Hypothesis-specific review.
elements.sent-2411-040: By the establishment of Metric, Proposal Agent can consequently receive the desired review of Features from Review Agent.
elements.sent-2411-041: Feedback is the review of the review that is conducted by Review Agent, focusing mainly on whether the review has identified the appropriate features and provided a sufficient summary of the results.
elements.sent-2411-042: Through Feedback, we aim to promote mutual enhancement between agents, hoping that they can facilitate this fully self-evolving process.
elements.sent-2411-043: Built upon the aforementioned components, Proposal Agent puts forward a comprehensive yet highly executable proposal, which is then submitted to the experiment module for execution.
elements.sent-2411-044: Upon receiving the review form Review Agent, as well as detailed experiment logs and results, Proposal Agent can initiate the next iteration, either of generating a brand new proposal to verify the new idea or modifying the current proposal to optimize the experiment results.
elements.sent-2411-045: Through this iterative process, we expect Proposal Agent to gradually evolve on the continuous trial and error, and eventually develop a scientific understanding of the given task.
elements.sent-2411-046: Drawing inspiration from human research practices, we recognize that significant insights and breakthroughs often emerge from in-depth analysis and reflection on experimental results.
elements.sent-2411-047: To facilitate this process, we designed Review Agent to analyze experiments and provide feedback to the Proposal Agent, with the goal of iteratively improving the quality of proposed hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-048: Aiming at a comprehensive and constructive review, the Review Agent conducts analysis at different levels of granularity.
elements.sent-2411-049: For coarse-grained analysis, it evaluates the overall performance of proposed hypothesis, providing feedback by comparing current experimental results against baselines.
elements.sent-2411-050: The performance review assesses current outcomes and suggests directions for potential improvements.
elements.sent-2411-051: For fine-grained analysis, Review Agent examines comprehensive experiment logs, analyzing intermediate results from multiple perspectives using both human-proposed and model-generated features.\textcolor{red {[TODO: CHECK THIS] The feature review identifies hidden patterns in the details, recommending more targeted adjustments to the proposed hypothesis.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Recognizing the importance of progressing from improved experiment results to scientific principles, we introduce Ablation Agent in our AIGS system.
elements.sent-2411-053: Ablation Agent has access to experiment records, including the proposals put forward by Proposal Agent, the experiment results from the experiment module, and the reviews generated by the Review Agent.
elements.sent-2411-054: Provided with these information, it is required to first identify the reasons behind the improvement of the experiment results, and then formulate a list of the scientific discoveries based on these insights.
elements.sent-2411-055: Finally, Ablation Agent need to propose the ablation studies, similar to the proposal generated by Proposal Agent, representing what it regard as essential in pursue of validating its discoveries.
elements.sent-2411-056: In that Ablation Agent must first identify the reasons for the improvement, we begin with picking out the iterations with significant advancement in the experiment result.
elements.sent-2411-057: For a certain iteration that exhibits improvement, we also include the baseline iteration, as specified in the Setting of proposal, into the analysis pool.
elements.sent-2411-058: This process is processed iteratively, until an iteration with a zero as baseline is visited.
elements.sent-2411-059: These iterations collectively form a chain and serve as the experiment record set which is provided to Ablation Agent.
elements.sent-2411-060: We conclude the rationale behind such practice as follows: we make the assumptions that each iteration chain represents the thought process of Proposal Agent and the improvement in experiment results should be attributed to the entire chain instead of a certain iteration.
elements.sent-2411-061: Therefore, we suppose that the full iteration chain is necessary for Ablation Agent to complete the given task.
elements.sent-2411-062: After giving the scientific discoveries concluded based on the combined information, Ablation Agent is tasked with generating the proposals for ablation studies.
elements.sent-2411-063: We require one proposal for each discovery, to ensure that the ablation study can focus on the verification of a single principle.
elements.sent-2411-064: Specifically, Ablation Agent must select an iteration as the baseline for the ablation study, and in the proposal generated by Ablation Agent, only Methodology and DSL are required, for Setting is predetermined to follow that of the baseline iteration.. Also.
elements.sent-2411-065: Targeting at a robust and reliable conclusion of the ablation study, both baseline and ablation experiments are repeatedly conducted multiple times.
elements.sent-2411-066: Ablation Agent is given the full record of these experiments, to decide the validity of the associated scientific principle.
elements.sent-2411-067: If a particular discovery withstands this process and consistently produces results similar to those in the main experiment, it is regarded as a verified and valuable scientific discovery.

elements.sent-2411-069: Rapid development of artificial intelligence has drastically accelerated the development of scientific discovery.
elements.sent-2411-070: Trained with large-scale observation data, deep neural networks extract the underlying patterns in an end-to-end manner and assist human researchers with highly-precised predictions in unseen scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-071: The recent rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) and the empowered autonomous agents enable scientists to gain help through interaction in different stages of their research, including but not limited to literature review, research ideation, idea implementation, and academic writing.
elements.sent-2411-072: However, AI researchers instantiated by foundation model empowered agents with full-process autonomy are still in their infancy.
elements.sent-2411-073: In this paper, we study \AIGSFull (\AIGS), where agents independently and autonomously complete the entire research process and discover scientific laws.
elements.sent-2411-074: By revisiting the definition of scientific research~\citep{Popper1935-POPTLO-7 , we argue that falsification is the essence of both human research process and the design of an \AIGS system.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Through the lens of falsification, prior systems attempting towards \AIGSFull either lack the part in their design, or rely heavily on existing verification engines that narrow the use in specialized domains.
elements.sent-2411-076: In this work, we propose \AIGSSys as a baby-step demonstration of a full-process \AIGS system, which is a multi-agent system with agents in roles representing key research process.
elements.sent-2411-077: By introducing \FalsificationAgent, which identify and then verify possible scientific discoveries, we empower the system with explicit falsification.
elements.sent-2411-078: Experiments on three tasks preliminarily show that \AIGSSys could produce meaningful scientific discoveries, though not on par with experienced human researchers.
elements.sent-2411-079: Finally, we discuss on the limitations of current \AIGSSys, actionable insights, and related ethical issues in detail.\footnote{Official Website: \url{https://agent-force.github.io/AIGS/ .}}

elements.sent-2411-082: Leveraging the enormous amount of experimental data, deep learning methods extract the underlying patterns in an end-to-end manner and effectively generalize to unobserved scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-083: The breakthroughs from deep learning in scientific domains, such as protein structure prediction~\citep{jumper2021highly , gravitational wave detection~\citep{george2018deep , and plasma control~\citep{degrave2022magnetic , have received award-winning recognition.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: As a result, AI for Science has emerged as a highly-regarded research field~\citep{wang2023scientific .}
elements.sent-2411-085: In the paradigm of AI for Science, AI primarily serves as a tool to assist researchers in making discoveries.
elements.sent-2411-086: With the rapid development of foundation models and autonomous agents~\citep{park2023generative , AI techniques nowadays boast the capabilities of general-purposed textual understanding and autonomous interaction with the external world.}
elements.sent-2411-087: These capabilities lead to the successful applications of AI-as-research-assistants, ranging from single-cell analysis~\citep{llmcell to drug discovery~\citep{llm-drug .}}
elements.sent-2411-088: The capability of providing research assistance leads to a more ambitious challenge: {\it Can foundation model-powered agents be autonomous researchers, independently completing the entire process of scientific discovery, thereby transforming AI for Science into \AIGSFull (\AIGS) ? \looseness=-1 When constructing an \AIGS system with full-process autonomy, the desiderata of the system design should refer to the definition of the scientific research process itself.}
elements.sent-2411-089: As stated by~\citet{Popper1935-POPTLO-7 , scientific research follows a systematic process of proposing novel hypotheses, conducting experiments through trial and error, and falsifying these hypotheses to conclude.}
elements.sent-2411-090: While it is widely-believed that creativity is indispensable in the process of research - which is also accounted in previous work~\citep{si2024can - the central component of scientific research is falsification: designing and executing experiments to validate or refute hypotheses, and falsified hypotheses pose positive contributions to scientific progress as well\footnote{\url{https://ml-retrospectives.github.io/ .. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Moreover, experienced researchers accumulate practical skills or reusable workflows~\citep{scientific-workflow from hands-on experimentation, which eases the design and execution of experiments and hypothesis falsification.}
elements.sent-2411-092: The abstraction of workflows in experiments enables effective reuse, which reflects a high level of executability in scientific research.
elements.sent-2411-093: To recapitulate, a creative idea is the beginning of a piece of scientific research, which is followed by experiments and analyses to be conducted; executability forms the basis for falsification, and a sequence of logically consistent falsification processes turns a novel idea into scientific discoveries with genuine creativity.
elements.sent-2411-094: As a result, falsification is the foundation of \AIGSFull, pillared by experimenting scaffolds accounting for executability and targeting at the ultimate goal of research creativity.
elements.sent-2411-095: In the first line, researchers evaluate and improve the capability of LLMs to generate research ideas with high creativity~\citep{si2024can,hu2024nova .}

elements.section-2411-015: The Development of AI-Accelerated Scientific Discovery
elements.sent-2411-112: In this section, we review and envision the development of AI-accelerated scientific discovery as four paradigms (Figure~\ref{fig:four-stages ): (I) AI as a Performance Optimizer, where deep neural networks are trained with large-scale observation data in a specific scientific problem to extract the patterns in an end-to-end manner.}
elements.sent-2411-113: In this paradigm, the AI techniques are used to optimize the specific prediction / regression performance in the pre-defined scientific problem with the consideration of out-of-domain generalization.
elements.sent-2411-114: The synergy between Paradigm (I) and (II) forms the AI-powered acceleration of scientific discovery nowadays.
elements.sent-2411-115: In this regime, foundation model empowered agents with scientist-like behavior should complete the entire research process, ranging from the initial idea proposal to the ultimate delivery of the scientific findings.
elements.sent-2411-116: Upon the prosperity of fully-autonomous AI researchers depicted in the previous stage, we envision the collaborations among the agentic researchers foster an AI-formed research community.

elements.subsection-2411-033: AI as a Performance Optimizer: Discoveries in Specific Tasks

elements.sent-2411-164: In this section, we elaborate how a baby-step system towards the full-process \AIGS is designed, in terms of design principles, overall system design, and detailed implementations.
elements.subsection-2411-037: Design Principles of a Full-Process \AIGS System
elements.sent-2411-165: The typical research process for human scientists~\citep{Popper1935-POPTLO-7 generally consists of two main stages: the pre-falsification stage, which encompasses exploration of research ideas, refinement of methodologies, and theoretical or empirical analysis, and the falsification stage, which involves hypothesizing scientific laws and validating these hypotheses based on theoretical or empirical findings.}
elements.sent-2411-166: In research fields like machine learning, empirical results for falsification process, i.e. ablation studies, are collected after researchers design and build a system, and conduct experiments.
elements.sent-2411-167: In contrast, other fields operate differently.
elements.sent-2411-168: For example, in physics or biology, empirical results are gathered from instruments or equipment after the experimental design and execution, while in mathematics or the humanities, theoretical insights are often derived through logical reasoning or literature review rather than empirical experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-169: These root falsification processes of different subjects in distinct knowledge source.

elements.sent-2411-322: Evaluators assess the falsification process based on three key dimensions, each scored on a scale from 0 to 2, with a higher score indicating better performance:.
elements.sent-2411-323: Importance Score: This score reflects the importance of the scientific discovery candidate.
elements.sent-2411-324: It evaluates the extent to which the identified factors can influence the experimental results, considering their relevance and potential impact with the primary experiments.
elements.sent-2411-325: Consistency Score: This score assesses whether the proposed ablation experiment plan is aligned with the identified scientific discovery candidate.
elements.sent-2411-326: It considers whether the experiments are designed to ablate the factor of interest and appropriately test the hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-327: Correctness Score: This score evaluates the accuracy of the final scientific discovery derived from the ablation studies.
elements.sent-2411-328: It considers whether the conclusions drawn from the ablation experiments and baseline results are correct, based on the observed empirical results.

elements.sent-2411-376: Envisioning the future of \AIGSFull systems powered by foundation models in real-world, in this section, we enumerate a few limitations for current \AIGSSys system and provide insights on the next steps of research for \AIGS.
elements.paragraph-2411-018: Balance idea diversity and system executability.
elements.sent-2411-377: As discussed in Section~\ref{sec:dsl , the design of the \DSL enhances the system executability but may constrain the idea diversity.}
elements.sent-2411-378: Achieving a balance between idea diversity and system executability requires further empirical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-379: One potential avenue is enabling agents to develop their own DSLs, which could enhance the executability of generated ideas without diminishing their diverse potential.
elements.paragraph-2411-019: Establish systematic mechanisms for evaluation and feedback.
elements.sent-2411-380: The quality of \AIGS system depends heavily on rigorous evaluation of prior proposals, methods, and results.
elements.sent-2411-381: Current approaches often adopt a peer review format, leveraging LLMs to generate feedback on results and guide future optimization~\citep{lu2024aiscientist, yu2024automated, jin2024agentreview .}
elements.sent-2411-382: However, it remains unclear whether this method is the most effective for large-scale research settings.
elements.sent-2411-383: Future work should explore systematic mechanisms to analyze outcomes across iterations, maximizing experience transfer and continuous improvement.
elements.sent-2411-384: Our research underscores the importance of falsification to enhance the scientific rigor of the research findings.
elements.sent-2411-385: While we have prototyped the falsification process in our \AIGSSys system, more efforts are required to strengthen the modules related to knowledge falsification, including the exploitation of the patterns and relationships derived from historical experiments for the guidance of refined research proposals.
elements.sent-2411-386: Besides, it is also vital for \AIGS systems to investigate whether the delivered new scientific knowledge could generalize across diverse research domains in an autonomous manner.
elements.paragraph-2411-021: Expand channels for scientific knowledge dissemination.
elements.sent-2411-387: Facilitating the exchange of \AIGSFull is critical, both between humans and AI and among AI systems.
elements.sent-2411-388: While \citet{lu2024aiscientist focus on disseminating knowledge through research papers, alternative formats like posters, podcasts, and videos are gaining traction with the rise of multi-modal agents.}
elements.sent-2411-389: Future research should also explore more efficient communication channels between AI systems, beyond structured text or natural language~\citep{pham2023let,chen2024beyond .}
elements.paragraph-2411-022: Exploring communication dynamics among autonomous AI researchers.
elements.sent-2411-390: As discussed in Section~\ref{sec:literaturereview , the advancement of AI-accelerated scientific discovery spans four paradigms, culminating in the emergence of an autonomous AI research community (Paradigm IV).}
elements.sent-2411-391: Within this community, individual agentic researchers engage in interactions that parallel collaborative dynamics found in human scientific networks.
elements.sent-2411-392: Analyzing these communication dynamics is essential to understand how fully-autonomous AI agents might effectively collaborate, exchange knowledge, and drive collective progress.
elements.sent-2411-393: In particular, a deeper exploration of these interactions in a multi-agent system will help establish communication frameworks that support optimal collaboration, fostering a robust and productive AI-accelerated research community.
elements.paragraph-2411-023: Promote interdisciplinary knowledge integration and experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-394: In this work, we primarily focused on the application of \AIGS systems within the domain of machine learning, where experiments could be executed in computers.
elements.sent-2411-395: However, future developments should extend these systems to address challenges in other scientific fields, such as biology, which has been preliminarily explored in a concurrent work~\citep{Swanson2024.11.11.623004 , chemistry, and physics, where cross-disciplinary knowledge integration is often crucial.}
elements.sent-2411-396: One major challenge lies in how AI agents can synthesize and align domain-specific knowledge from multiple fields, which often have distinct terminologies, methodologies, and epistemological assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-397: Another critical challenge is the experiment environment, which could be hardly automated and might be highly resource-consuming.
elements.sent-2411-398: We hope the integrity and development of optional modules like Domain-specific ExpAgent and EnvironmentAgent mentioned in Section~\ref{sec:overall-system could alleviate the challenges, and further effort is needed and will be made in future work.}
elements.sent-2411-399: In our \AIGSSys system, the agent did not perform harmful operations on computer systems or environment because of the design of \DSL, task constraints and no access to external tools.
elements.sent-2411-400: However, while the system developed in this study is limited in scope, \AIGS systems as a whole may have significant impacts in the future, with potential risks that should not be overlooked.
elements.sent-2411-401: This section explores the potential negative impacts of such systems, drawing on prior research, and offers suggestions for promoting their positive development.
elements.paragraph-2411-024: Impact on Human Researchers and Academic Community
elements.sent-2411-402: In the absence of robust publication standards and academic review processes, \AIGS systems could flood the academic community with low-quality literature, which will further increase researchers' workload and disrupt the efficient dissemination of knowledge~\citep{lu2024aiscientist, si2024can, hu2024nova .}
elements.sent-2411-403: And although \citet{si2024can and \citet{kumar2024can suggest that LLMs can generate ideas more creative than humans, the extent of such creativity remains uncertain.}}
elements.sent-2411-404: LLM-powered \AIGS systems tend to rely heavily on existing data and patterns, which could foster path dependency and limit opportunities for groundbreaking discoveries.
elements.sent-2411-405: Additionally, these systems might inadvertently use proprietary or copyrighted material, raising concerns about intellectual property infringement~\citep{kumar2024can .}
elements.sent-2411-406: Furthermore, \AIGS systems also present several unpredictable challenges for human researchers:.
elements.sent-2411-407: Dependence Effect and Cognitive Inertia: Over-reliance on AI-generated insights may diminish researchers’ independent thinking, leading to cognitive stagnation and a decline in critical thinking skills~\citep{si2024can, hu2024nova .}
elements.sent-2411-408: Ambiguity in Responsibility Attribution: The involvement of AI complicates the assignment of credit and responsibility, potentially disrupting existing incentive structure~\citep{si2024can, hu2024nova .}
elements.sent-2411-409: Weakened Collaboration and Increased Isolation: As \AIGS systems become capable of independently generating publishable work, researchers may increasingly rely on these systems, reducing the need for direct collaboration and communication with colleagues.
elements.sent-2411-410: This shift could lead to a decline in interpersonal interaction, weakening traditional research networks built on teamwork and shared discourse~\citep{si2024can, hu2024nova .}
elements.sent-2411-411: Over time, the diminishing frequency of collaborative exchanges may foster a sense of professional isolation among human researchers, heightening the risk of loneliness, disengagement, and reduced psychological well-being.
elements.sent-2411-412: Exacerbated Technological Barriers: Without equitable access to advanced \AIGS systems, a technological divide could emerge, disadvantaging researchers unfamiliar with or lacking access to these systems, thereby exacerbating inequalities within the community.
elements.sent-2411-413: This inefficiency, caused by limited reasoning capabilities, the misuse of erroneous information, or ambiguity in task definition, could drive up energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-414: Moreover, poorly regulated experiments, especially without adequate simulation environments, can lead to unintended environmental harm.
elements.sent-2411-415: For example, untested chemical processes in materials science may yield hazardous by-products, while unchecked experiments in nuclear research could increase the risk of radiation leaks~\citep{tang2024prioritizing .}
elements.sent-2411-416: This raises concerns about their misuse for harmful purposes, such as designing more advanced adversarial attack strategies~\citep{tang2024prioritizing, si2024can, lu2024aiscientist, kumar2024can, hu2024nova .}
elements.sent-2411-417: Even with benign intentions, unsupervised scientific research may introduce unforeseen societal risks.
elements.sent-2411-418: For instance, monopolizing breakthroughs in autonomous AI could lead to severe unemployment, market monopolies, and social unrest~\citep{tang2024prioritizing .}
elements.subsection-2411-044: Strategies for Responsible and Ethical Development of Automated Research Systems
elements.paragraph-2411-027: Strengthening the Security of Foundation Models
elements.sent-2411-419: The most fundamental step in mitigating security risks associated with \AIGS systems is enhancing the security of their foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-420: Incorporating instructions for handling unsafe research into the alignment training corpus, alongside conducting rigorous safety audits prior to model deployment, are both crucial strategies to ensure the systems be robust and secure~\citep{tang2024prioritizing .}
elements.paragraph-2411-028: Aligning Scientific Agents with Human Intentions, Environment and Self-constraints
elements.sent-2411-421: Scientific agents in \AIGS systems should align with human intentions, the environments in which they operate, and self-constraints~\citep{yang2024towards .}
elements.sent-2411-422: Human Intentions: Agents must accurately interpret user intent, going beyond literal language to capture the deeper purpose of scientific inquiries.
elements.sent-2411-423: Environment: Agents need to adapt to the specific environments in which they function by applying domain-specific knowledge accurately and utilizing specialized tools effectively.
elements.sent-2411-424: Self-Constraints: Agents must evaluate task feasibility, manage resources wisely, and minimize waste to ensure sustainable operation.
elements.sent-2411-425: This includes setting boundaries to prevent redundant work or harmful behavior, which is essential for maintaining system efficiency.
elements.paragraph-2411-029: Providing Comprehensive Training for Human Users
elements.sent-2411-426: Comprehensive and rigorous training is essential for users to fully leverage \AIGS systems and prevent unintended consequences~\citep{aisdpi .}
elements.sent-2411-427: Proper training minimizes the risk of misuse that could lead to environmental harm, resource waste, or unethical research outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-428: Training programs should focus not only on technical skills but also on ethical considerations, ensuring users understand the limitations and responsibilities associated with these systems~\citep{tang2024prioritizing .}
elements.paragraph-2411-030: Building a Collaborative Framework Between Automated Research Systems and Human Researchers
elements.sent-2411-429: To prevent \AIGS systems from exerting excessive influence on the academic community, collaboration between \AIGS systems and human researchers will play a crucial role~\citep{si2024can, hu2024nova .}
elements.sent-2411-430: It is essential to explore the new roles and responsibilities that human scientists may need to assume in this evolving research landscape shaped by the presence of \AIGS systems.
elements.sent-2411-431: A well-structured partnership can leverage the complementary strengths of both, enabling outcomes that neither could achieve independently.
elements.sent-2411-432: Moreover, such collaboration fosters interaction among human researchers, encouraging deeper communication and mitigating the sense of isolation that may arise from increased reliance on automated tools.
elements.paragraph-2411-031: Establishing Comprehensive Legal and Accountability Frameworks
elements.sent-2411-433: A robust legal and accountability framework is crucial to govern the use of \AIGS systems.. This framework should:.
elements.sent-2411-434: Define Clear Scientific Research Boundaries: Specify the permissible scope and limitations of these systems, where regulate agents with the \DSL might be helpful.
elements.sent-2411-435: Clarify Responsibility and Credit Allocation: Establish guidelines for assigning credit and responsibility for research outcomes generated with the assistance of \AIGS systems~\citep{si2024can, hu2024nova .}
elements.sent-2411-436: Implement Penalties for Misuse: Outline liability measures and penalties to address harmful behavior or unethical practices involving these systems.
elements.paragraph-2411-032: Using \AIGS Systems to Address Its Own Challenges
elements.sent-2411-437: For example, \AIGS systems could be used to monitor and evaluate outputs from other automated systems, identifying potential ethical issues, biases, or environmental risks before they escalate.
elements.sent-2411-438: Moreover, \AIGS systems can facilitate the development of guidelines, by automating the analysis of research trends and regulatory needs, thus helping shape future policies for responsible AI use.
elements.sent-2411-439: When employed strategically, \AIGS systems become not only tools for discovery but also mechanisms for self-regulation, creating a virtuous cycle of innovation and governance.
elements.sent-2411-440: We introduce the concept of \AIGS in this paper and implement \AIGSSys, a baby-step toward full-process automated scientific discovery systems, with a focus on incorporating falsification into the research process.
elements.sent-2411-441: By integrating a \FalsificationAgent, the multi-agent system can identify and verify potential discoveries.
elements.sent-2411-442: Techniques as \DSL and multi-sampling strategy are introduced for two other principles of \AIGS systems design, executability and creativity.
elements.sent-2411-443: Preliminary experiments show promise, though the system's performance remains below that of experienced human researchers.
elements.sent-2411-444: This work lays the groundwork for future developments in \AIGS systems, with further improvements over \AIGSSys and ethical considerations necessary for advancing the field.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2025_conference .}
elements.sent-2411-445: In this section, we elaborate the implementation details of the \AIGSSys system.
elements.sent-2411-446: All artifacts are used as intended with their license strictly followed in our work.

elements.sent-2411-448: For \ExperimentAgent, given the design of \DSL with human effort, proposed methodology generated by \ProposalAgent can be executed reliably in experiments, which is also shown in Section~\ref{tab:success_rate .}
elements.sent-2411-449: This reduces the need of iteratively refining proposals between \ProposalAgent and \ExperimentAgent.
elements.sent-2411-450: For \LiteratureAgent, preliminary results show literature integration did not significantly impact the outcomes in both phases of \AIGSSys.
